# Checking your argument

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## In a nutshell
- Once you have a draft, three tests reveal whether your argument holds together
- They work at increasing levels of scale: paragraph, paper, plain language
:::

You have your funnel. You know what your anchor theory is and what it predicts. Your paper sits in a field full of other theories, and you need to handle them without letting them take over. The two-sentence check in the previous chapter gave you a principled basis for those decisions before you started writing. This chapter gives you three tests to apply once you have a draft.

## The argument progression test

A well-written theoretical framing is a sequence of steps, each of which advances the argument by exactly one move. For each paragraph in your theoretical framing, ask: what single step does this paragraph add to my argument? Write it down in one sentence. If you cannot, the paragraph needs to be revised or removed.

Then read your one-sentence summaries in sequence. They should tell the complete story of your theoretical argument -- from phenomenon to prediction -- without gaps or redundancy. A gap means a step is missing. Redundancy means two paragraphs are doing the same job, and one of them should go.

This is the paragraph-level version of the anchor sentence. The anchor sentence compresses your entire theoretical commitment into one sentence. The argument progression test checks whether every paragraph is doing real work on the way to that sentence.

## The elevator abstract

Once the argument holds paragraph by paragraph, try to compress the whole paper into two sentences: one stating what you did and why, one stating what you found and what it means. Do not open your manuscript while you do this. A contribution that cannot be stated in two sentences without opening the manuscript is not yet clear enough to publish.

A weak elevator abstract:

> We studied event boundaries and surprise ratings in video clips. We found that surprise was lower at boundaries, which is consistent with Event Segmentation Theory.

A strong elevator abstract:

> We tested the prediction of Event Segmentation Theory that boundary detection reduces prediction error, using surprise ratings as a direct measure of that error across boundary and within-event pause points in everyday activity clips. Surprise was reliably lower at boundaries, supporting the specific claim that event model updating -- not narrative goal structure -- drives the reduction.

## The plain-language summary

In Chapter 2, you produced a plain-language summary before writing. Return to it now and read it alongside your draft.

The two will sound different because a scientific paper and a plain-language summary are different documents. What matters is whether they are about the same thing. Academic writing accumulates qualifications, hedges, and theoretical scaffolding until the original point is no longer visible. If your plain-language summary describes a study that your draft no longer clearly resembles, the draft has drifted.

The plain-language summary does not change. The draft does.
